title: 10 best herbal teas for viral hepatitis

teas.tea_desc: Milk thistle tea assists with viral hepatitis due to its active component, silymarin. This flavonoid possesses antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that directly counter viral hepatitis. Specifically, silymarin inhibits the replication of hepatitis C virus and encourages liver cell regeneration. Moreover, it protects liver cells by preventing the absorption of toxins and reducing oxidative stress. Finally, milk thistle tea bolsters the immune system, further reinforcing the body's defense against hepatitis viruses.
tea_parts: Seeds: The seeds of the milk thistle plant contain silymarin, a flavonoid complex with antiviral properties that support liver health and help manage viral hepatitis symptoms.
Leaves: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, milk thistle leaves help protect liver cells from damage and aid in detoxification, benefiting those with viral hepatitis.
Flowers: High in protective antioxidants, milk thistle flowers support liver function and promote cell regeneration, easing the impact of viral hepatitis on the body.
tea_recipe: Measure - Use a teaspoon to measure out 1-2 grams of dried milk thistle seeds or 1 tea bag of milk thistle tea.
Boil - Boil 8 ounces of water in a teapot or saucepan on the stove.
Steep - Steep the milk thistle seeds or tea bag in the boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain - Strain the milk thistle tea to remove the seeds or tea bag, if applicable.
Serve - Enjoy the warm milk thistle tea, ideally 2-3 times a day for viral hepatitis symptoms.
tea_constituents: Silymarin: This is the most well-known medicinal constituent of milk thistle, and it has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that can help protect the liver from damage caused by viral hepatitis.

Silybin: A flavonoid that is a major active component of silymarin, silybin has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, as well as the ability to inhibit hepatitis C virus replication.
Silydianin: Another flavonoid found in milk thistle, silydianin has been shown to have antiviral properties, specifically against hepatitis B virus.
Silychristin: A flavonolignan, silychristin has been shown to have antioxidant properties and to inhibit the replication of hepatitis C virus.
Isosilybin: A flavonolignan, isosilybin has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, and it can help protect liver cells from damage caused by viral hepatitis.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea aids viral hepatitis due to its proven antiviral and antioxidant properties. This herbal tea, derived from Taraxacum officinale, contains compounds such as taraxacin, taraxacerin, and caffeic acid that exhibit strong antiviral effects. These compounds inhibit the replication of hepatitis viruses, reducing viral load and promoting liver cell regeneration. Moreover, dandelion tea's high antioxidant content protects liver cells from oxidative stress and inflammation, further supporting liver health. Lastly, dandelion tea stimulates bile production and enhances liver function, improving overall liver health and accelerating recovery from hepatitis.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of dandelions are used to make medicinal tea for viral hepatitis due to their proven antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that help in reducing liver inflammation.
Leaves: Dandelion leaves are rich in antioxidants and have been shown to improve liver function, making them a crucial part of the tea.
Flowers: The flowers of dandelions contain bioactive compounds that support liver health and are used in making medicinal tea to treat viral hepatitis.
Buds: Dandelion buds are a powerhouse of nutrients, including vitamins and minerals that aid in the detoxification process, making them an essential part of the tea.
tea_recipe: Gather 2-3 dandelion heads and rinse them thoroughly under running water to remove any dirt or debris.
Separate the yellow petals from the green base and stems, then measure out 1 tablespoon of petals.
Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool down for a minute before adding the dandelion petals.
Steep the petals in hot water for 5-10 minutes, then strain out the petals and discard them.
Drink the dandelion tea 2-3 times a day, preferably between meals, for at least a month to help support liver function during viral hepatitis.
tea_constituents: Taraxacin: A anti-inflammatory compound that may help reduce liver inflammation caused by viral hepatitis.
Taraxasterol: A compound that may help improve liver function and reduce liver damage caused by viral hepatitis.
Chicoric acid: A antioxidant that may help protect the liver from oxidative stress caused by viral hepatitis.
Luteolin: A flavonoid that may have antiviral properties and help reduce viral replication in cases of viral hepatitis.
Caffeic acid: A phenolic acid that may have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, helping to reduce symptoms of viral hepatitis.
Ferulic acid: A antioxidant that may help protect the liver from oxidative stress and reduce inflammation caused by viral hepatitis.
Chlorogenic acid: A antioxidant that may help reduce liver inflammation and improve liver function in cases of viral hepatitis.

teas.tea_desc: Chicory tea assists with viral hepatitis due to its anti-inflammatory properties, derived from its main component, chicoric acid. This acid reduces inflammation by inhibiting the production of inflammatory cytokines, aiding in the alleviation of liver inflammation caused by the hepatitis virus. Chicory tea also exhibits antioxidant qualities that help protect liver cells from oxidative stress and damage, further supporting liver health during viral hepatitis. Moreover, chicory tea stimulates bile production, promoting digestion and the elimination of toxins from the body, which is beneficial for overall liver function. Lastly, chicory tea demonstrates antiviral properties, potentially inhibiting the replication of the hepatitis virus, thus contributing to an enhanced recovery process.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in inulin, a type of dietary fiber that aids the liver in detoxification and supports overall liver health.
Leaves: Contain high levels of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that help reduce inflammation and protect liver cells from damage due to viral hepatitis.
Flowers: Packed with flavonoids, which have potent anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties that can help inhibit the replication of hepatitis viruses.
Seeds: A natural source of essential fatty acids that support liver function and have been shown to decrease oxidative stress in liver tissues.
tea_recipe: Chop: Slice 50 grams of dried chicory root into small pieces, taking approximately 5 minutes.
Boil: Pour 1 liter of water into a pot and bring it to a boil, which should take around 10 minutes.
Steep: Add the chopped chicory root to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain: Pour the tea through a fine-mesh strainer into a teapot or mug, discarding the solids, and allow it to cool for 5 minutes.
Serve: Drink 2-3 cups of this chicory tea daily, either hot or cold, as a potential complementary treatment for viral hepatitis.
tea_constituents: Inulin: A type of fiber that can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the liver, which is beneficial for people with viral hepatitis.
Cichoric acid: A polyphenol that has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect the liver from damage caused by viral hepatitis.
Sesquiterpene lactones: A group of compounds that have been shown to have antiviral properties, which may help inhibit the replication of the hepatitis virus.
Tannins: A type of polyphenolic compound that has anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, which can help reduce liver inflammation and inhibit the growth of the hepatitis virus.
Flavonoids: A class of compounds with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help protect the liver from damage caused by viral hepatitis.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea offers relief for viral hepatitis due to its antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, derived from the menthol and rosmarinic acid it contains. These compounds inhibit the replication of viruses while reducing inflammation in the liver. Furthermore, peppermint tea stimulates the production of bile, aiding in digestion and detoxification. Its antioxidants neutralize free radicals, preventing further liver damage. Additionally, peppermint tea's calming effects on the gastrointestinal tract alleviate symptoms such as nausea and vomiting associated with viral hepatitis.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the peppermint plant contain rosmarinic acid, which possesses antiviral properties that can help in treating viral hepatitis.
Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant are rich in essential oils like menthol and menthone, which have been shown to have hepatoprotective effects.
Flowers: The flowers of the peppermint plant contain flavonoids that exhibit antiviral activities, making them effective in treating viral hepatitis.
Seeds: The seeds of the peppermint plant are a treasure trove of antioxidants, which can help in managing oxidative stress caused by viral hepatitis.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 2 teaspoons of dried peppermint leaves with 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the mixture, covered, for 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors to infuse.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, pouring it into a cup.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Sip the peppermint tea slowly, aiming to consume 2-3 cups per day.
tea_constituents: Menthol: Has antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce viral replication and inflammation in the liver associated with viral hepatitis.
Rosmarinic acid: Exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help protect liver cells from oxidative damage and inflammation caused by viral hepatitis.
Flavonoids: Possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties, which can help protect liver cells, reduce inflammation, and inhibit viral replication.
Tannins: Exhibit astringent and antiviral properties, which can help protect liver cells from damage and reduce viral replication in viral hepatitis.
Essential oils: Contain antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties, which can help protect liver cells, reduce inflammation, and inhibit viral replication.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea assists with viral hepatitis due to its antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties. The active components in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, inhibit the replication of viral cells. Moreover, ginger tea stimulates the production of bile, aiding in digestion and detoxification. Additionally, ginger's antioxidant properties help reduce oxidative stress in liver cells, while its anti-inflammatory effects alleviate inflammation caused by hepatitis. Consequently, ginger tea supports liver function and overall well-being during viral hepatitis.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the ginger plant contain antiviral properties that can inhibit the replication of the hepatitis virus.
Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are rich in antioxidants that help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the liver caused by viral hepatitis.
Leaves: The leaves of the ginger plant have high levels of vitamins and minerals that support liver function and aid in the detoxification process during viral hepatitis.
Flowers: Ginger flowers contain potent anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce liver inflammation and swelling caused by viral hepatitis.
Seeds: Ginger seeds are a natural antiviral agent that can help reduce the viral load in patients with viral hepatitis.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger; place in a teapot and add 2 cups of boiling water.
Steep the ginger in hot water for 10 minutes, then strain the tea to remove solids.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of honey and 1/2 lemon juice to the ginger tea for flavor and vitamin C.
Warm the ginger tea on low heat until it's comfortably hot, but not boiling.
Pour the ginger tea into a cup and enjoy 2-3 times daily to soothe your throat and support liver health.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols have potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the liver, which are often associated with viral hepatitis.
Shogaols: Shogaols exhibit strong antiviral activities, which may help inhibit the replication of viral hepatitis in the body.
Paradols: Paradols have been shown to protect liver cells from damage caused by oxidative stress, thereby promoting liver health and recovery in viral hepatitis patients.
Zingerone: Zingerone has anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective effects, which can help alleviate liver inflammation and damage caused by viral hepatitis.

Ginger essential oil: Ginger essential oil contains several bioactive compounds that exhibit antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce liver damage and promote recovery in viral hepatitis.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea aids in treating viral hepatitis due to its primary component, glycyrrhizin. Glycyrrhizin inhibits viral replication, preventing the hepatitis virus from spreading further in the body. This compound also demonstrates anti-inflammatory properties, reducing liver inflammation caused by the virus. Moreover, licorice tea protects liver cells by promoting cell regeneration and enhancing the liver's overall function. Lastly, licorice tea's ability to boost the immune system enables the body to combat the hepatitis virus more effectively.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in glycyrrhizin, the root of licorice plant possesses anti-viral and anti-inflammatory properties making it effective in treating viral hepatitis.
Leaves: High in flavonoids, licorice leaves aid in liver protection and regeneration, beneficial for managing viral hepatitis.
Seeds: Packed with coumarins, licorice seeds improve liver function and reduce inflammation, beneficial for those suffering from viral hepatitis.
Flowers: Abundant in essential oils and glycosides, licorice flowers promote bile secretion and liver health, helpful in treating viral hepatitis.
tea_recipe: Boil 2 cups of water in a pot for 5 minutes.
Add 2 teaspoons of dried licorice root to the boiling water, then reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes.
Turn off heat, let the tea steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root, then let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 2-3 cups of licorice tea daily for several days to aid in viral hepatitis recovery.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This triterpenoid saponin in licorice tea has been shown to have antiviral effects against hepatitis viruses, inhibiting their replication and thus helping to reduce liver damage.
Liquiritin: This flavonoid in licorice tea has been shown to have hepatoprotective effects, reducing liver inflammation and oxidative stress, which can help alleviate symptoms of viral hepatitis.
Glabridin: This isoflavan in licorice tea has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help protect liver cells from damage caused by viral hepatitis.
Liquiritigenin: This flavonoid in licorice tea has been shown to have antiviral effects, particularly against the hepatitis C virus, inhibiting its replication and potentially reducing liver damage.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea offers several properties that benefit individuals with viral hepatitis. First, it contains powerful antioxidants that aid in liver protection and detoxification. Second, chamomile's anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the liver, a common symptom of hepatitis. Third, it boasts antiviral properties that directly combat hepatitis-causing viruses. Fourth, chamomile tea's calming effects help alleviate stress and anxiety associated with the disease. Lastly, this tea improves sleep quality, promoting overall well-being and accelerating the healing process.
tea_parts: Flowers: The chamomile flowers contain terpenoids and flavonoids that possess liver-protecting properties, making them ideal for medicinal tea to treat viral hepatitis.
Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, chamomile leaves help reduce oxidative stress and support liver function, making them crucial in medicinal tea for viral hepatitis.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are a natural source of essential oils and antioxidants that aid in digestion, reducing inflammation, and promoting liver health in medicinal tea for hepatitis.
tea_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water to 212 degrees Fahrenheit, then let cool for 2-3 minutes.
Pour the heated water into a teapot containing 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers.
Steep the chamomile flowers in the hot water for 5-10 minutes, depending on desired strength.
Strain the chamomile tea into a cup, discarding the flowers.
Drink the chamomile tea slowly, ideally between meals, for best absorption and potential liver benefits.
tea_constituents: Apigenin: This flavonoid has been shown to have anti-viral properties, which can help to combat the hepatitis virus.
Luteolin: A antioxidant flavonoid which helps in reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the liver, common symptoms of viral hepatitis.
Chamazulene: This compound has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce liver inflammation caused by the hepatitis virus.
Bisabolol: A terpene known for its anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antimicrobial properties, which can help to alleviate symptoms of viral hepatitis.
Matricin: A precursor to chamazulene, matricin has been shown to have hepatoprotective effects, meaning it can help to protect the liver from damage caused by the hepatitis virus.

teas.tea_desc: Burdock tea assists with viral hepatitis due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which originate from its main active compounds, including luteolin, quercetin, and sesquiterpenes. These compounds have demonstrated abilities to reduce oxidative stress, diminish inflammation, and protect liver cells from damage caused by hepatitis. Furthermore, burdock tea encourages the production of vital enzymes and substances that promote liver health, such as glutathione and superoxide dismutase. Traditional and contemporary applications of burdock tea also highlight its effectiveness in treating liver ailments, including viral hepatitis. Lastly, scientific research supports burdock tea's role in alleviating liver-related conditions, as studies illustrate its potential in decreasing liver enzyme levels, indicative of improved liver function.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of burdock contain anti-viral properties that can help in treating viral hepatitis.
Rhizomes: Burdock's rhizomes are rich in antioxidants that help in liver detoxification, thus beneficial in managing viral hepatitis.
Leaves: Burdock leaves are known for their anti-inflammatory and diuretic properties, which assist in soothing liver inflammation caused by viral hepatitis.
Seeds: The seeds of burdock are packed with anti-inflammatory compounds and have been used in treating various liver ailments, including hepatitis.
Buds: Burdock buds contain essential oils and tannins that protect the liver from damage and support its overall health, aiding in viral hepatitis management.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 2 cups of water and 2 tablespoons of dried burdock root, then bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the burdock root pieces.
Allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 2-3 cups of burdock tea daily for optimal results.
tea_constituents: Arctiin: Arctiin, a lignan found in burdock, has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective effects, which may help reduce liver inflammation caused by viral hepatitis.
Arctigenin: Arctigenin, another lignan in burdock, has been found to have antiviral properties, which can help inhibit the replication of hepatitis viruses.
Quercetin: Quercetin, a flavonoid present in burdock, has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help protect liver cells from damage caused by viral hepatitis.
Resveratrol: Resveratrol, a polyphenol found in burdock, has been found to have hepatoprotective effects, which can help reduce liver damage caused by viral hepatitis.

Sesquiterpene lactones: Sesquiterpene lactones in burdock, such as germacranolides and eudesmanolides, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, which can help regulate the immune response to viral hepatitis.

Volatile oils: Volatile oils in burdock, such as thiophene derivatives and aliphatic aldehydes, have been found to have antimicrobial and antiviral properties, which can help inhibit the growth of hepatitis viruses.

teas.tea_desc: Yellow dock tea contains bitter constituents that stimulate the production of bile, promoting digestion and the elimination of toxins. This herbal tea also exhibits antioxidant properties, helping to reduce oxidative stress on liver cells caused by viral hepatitis. Moreover, yellow dock's anti-inflammatory properties can alleviate inflammation in the liver, a common symptom of viral hepatitis. Additionally, yellow dock demonstrates antiviral effects, neutralizing the hepatitis virus and preventing its replication. Lastly, yellow dock's ability to support the immune system ensures the body has the resources to combat and recover from viral hepatitis.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in medicinal compounds, the roots of yellow dock are used in tea to promote liver health and assist in detoxification, benefiting those with viral hepatitis.
Rhizomes: High in anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, yellow dock rhizomes are included in tea to support liver function and soothe hepatitis-related inflammation.
Leaves: The leaves of yellow dock contain vitamins and minerals that, when consumed as a tea, help strengthen the immune system and aid in the body's response to viral hepatitis.
Flowers: Yellow dock flowers, when infused in tea, provide antiviral properties that help inhibit the replication of hepatitis viruses and support overall liver health.
tea_recipe: Chop: Slice 2-3 yellow dock roots ( washed and scrubbed) into small pieces.
Steep: Place the chopped yellow dock roots in a pot, add 4 cups of water, bring to a boil and then simmer for 20 minutes.
Strain: Pour the tea through a fine-mesh strainer into a cup, discarding the solids.
Customize (optional): Add honey or lemon to taste.
Serve: Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature and consume it in small sips throughout the day.
tea_constituents: Berberine: Berberine has been shown to have antiviral properties, which can help inhibit the replication of the hepatitis virus.
Resveratrol: Resveratrol has anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce liver inflammation associated with viral hepatitis.
Quercetin: Quercetin has antioxidant properties that can help protect liver cells from oxidative damage caused by the hepatitis virus.
Catechins: Catechins have antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help inhibit the replication of the hepatitis virus and reduce liver inflammation.

Chlorogenic Acid: Chlorogenic Acid has antioxidant properties that can help protect liver cells from oxidative damage caused by the hepatitis virus. It also has antiviral properties that can help inhibit the replication of the hepatitis virus.

teas.tea_desc: Schisandra tea's anti-inflammatory properties directly combat hepatitis-inducing viruses, protecting the liver by inhibiting the virus's ability to replicate. This tea's lignans also aid in liver cell regeneration, further countering the damage caused by the virus. Additionally, schisandra tea strengthens the immune system, equipping it to better fend off hepatitis. Lastly, schisandra tea's antioxidants counteract oxidative stress, a common side effect of viral hepatitis, thus helping to restore normal liver function.
tea_parts: Roots: Schisandra roots are used in medicinal tea due to their effectiveness in treating liver diseases, including viral hepatitis, thanks to their high content of lignans which possess potent antiviral properties.
Rhyzomes: Schisandra rhyzomes are used in teas for hepatitis treatment because they contain schisandrins which have been found to have protective effects on liver cells.
Seeds: Schisandra seeds are used in tea for viral hepatitis because they contain schisantherin A, a compound with demonstrated antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that help combat hepatitis.
Leaves: Schisandra leaves are suitable for making medicinal tea for viral hepatitis as they contain compounds like gomisin A that protect the liver from damage caused by the hepatitis virus.
Flowers: Schisandra flowers are utilized in teas to manage viral hepatitis symptoms due to their gomisin N content, which has been shown to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
tea_recipe: In a tea pot, combine 1 tablespoon of dried Schisandra berries with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Strain the tea to remove the solid berries, leaving only the liquid.
Optionally, add honey or lemon to taste for improved flavor.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for viral hepatitis support.
tea_constituents: Schisandrins: Schisandrins, a type of dibenzocyclooctadiene derivative, have been shown to possess potent antiviral activities against hepatitis C virus (HCV) by inhibiting viral replication and suppressing viral RNA and protein expression.
Schisantherin A: Schisantherin A, a lignan found in Schisandra, has demonstrated anti-hepatitis B virus (HBV) activity by suppressing HBV DNA replication and inhibiting HBsAg and HBeAg secretion.

Gomisin N: Gomisin N, a lignan isolated from Schisandra, has been reported to have hepatoprotective effects by reducing liver enzyme levels, suppressing lipid peroxidation, and increasing glutathione levels in liver tissues.
Deoxyschisandrin: Deoxyschisandrin, a dibenzocyclooctadiene derivative, has exhibited antiviral activity against HCV by inhibiting viral replication and reducing viral load.

Schisandrol B: Schisandrol B, a lignan, has been shown to have hepatoprotective effects by reducing liver enzyme levels, inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and increasing glutathione levels in liver tissues.
Schisanhenol: Schisanhenol, a lignan, has demonstrated antiviral activity against HCV by inhibiting viral replication and reducing viral load.

other_remedies: Vitamin and mineral supplements are the most common and effective natural remedies for viral hepatitis. Milk thistle, a herbal supplement, is sometimes used as it contains silymarin which has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help protect the liver. However, its effectiveness is still a subject of ongoing research. Probiotics, which are live bacteria and yeasts that are good for your health, especially your digestive system, can also be beneficial in managing viral hepatitis as they help restore the balance of bacteria in the gut and support the immune system. Additionally, a balanced diet that includes lean proteins, whole grains, fruits, and vegetables provides essential nutrients that support liver function and overall health. Regular exercise is also crucial in managing viral hepatitis as it helps the body eliminate toxins, reduces inflammation, and improves overall health.
intro: For those suffering from viral hepatitis, certain herbal teas have shown promise in alleviating symptoms and supporting liver health. Turmeric tea, rich in curcumin, has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce liver inflammation. Ginger tea, with its natural anti-inflammatory compounds, soothes the digestive system and improves liver function. Additionally, Peppermint tea's calming effects on the digestive tract help alleviate symptoms of nausea and vomiting often associated with hepatitis. Licorice root tea, with its antiviral properties, has been shown to inhibit the replication of hepatitis viruses. These herbal teas, when consumed as part of a holistic treatment plan, provide a natural and gentle approach to managing viral hepatitis.
definition: Viral hepatitis is a disease caused by several types of viruses, including Hepatitis A, B, C, D, and E, that inflame the liver, impairing its function and negatively affecting people's lives. In severe cases, viral hepatitis leads to chronic liver damage, liver cancer, and even death. Furthermore, people with viral hepatitis face social stigma, discrimination, and mental health issues due to the lack of understanding and awareness about the disease. As a result, the disease impacts not only individuals' physical health but also their mental and social well-being, leading to decreased quality of life.
